coredata.dc:title: Redox-controlled fluorescent sensor for highly reactive oxygen species detection and cell imaging based on dihydrolipoic acid functionalized carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ruxia
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: A novel redox-controlled fluorescent sensor for the detection of highly reactive oxygen species (hROS) was developed based on the unique green fluorescence emission of dihydrolipoic acid functionalized carbon quantum dots (DHLA@N-CQDs). Given the excellent redox characteristic between the disulfide bonds (S–S) and thiol (R-SH), the sensor was designed by bonding N-CQDs and DHLA under the action of coupling agents. When hROS was introduced into the sensing system, a large number of S–S gathered between DHLA@N-CQDs can effectively inhibit the fluorescence of probe. In addition, the as-prepared fluorescent sensor can sense hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in different response ranges, which ensures a high selectivity for hROS detection. Furthermore, the sensor system has been successfully utilized to detect the H2O2 in human saliva samples and the fluorescence imaging of ·OH in HeLa cells, exhibiting the feasibility of fluorescent sensors in practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Reactive oxygen species
Cell imaging
Redox
Dihydrolipoic acid
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing procedure for detection of hROS
Possible quenching mechanism of hROS fluorescence assay
Optimize the experimental parameters of the sensing procedure
Quantification of hROS using the ON-OFF sensing behavior of DHLA@N-CQDs
Selectivity and interference study for hROS detection
Detecting H2O2 in human saliva samples
Cytotoxicity test of DHLA@N-CQDs and its cellular imaging application

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, HRTEM (inset) imaging for DHLA@N-CQDs; (B) the size distribution for DHLA@N-CQDs; (C) FTIR spectrum of DHLA, N-CQDs, DHLA@N-CQDs; (D) UV–vis spectrum of DHLA@N-CQDs and N-CQDs and the inset was the pictures of DHLA@N-CQDs and N-CQDs under the UV dark box, respectively.
(A) XPS full-survey and high-resolution XPS of (B) S2p, (C) C1s, (D) N1s, (E) O1s for DHLA@N-CQDs.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum of DHLA@N-CQDs and N-CQDs, (B) Fluorescence emission spectrum of DHLA@N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths.
(A) The influence of different types of buffer solutions on the fluorescence sensing system in a series of pH ranges; (B) The influence of different incubation time on fluorescence sensing system.

(A) Fluorescence spectral in the presence of OH of varying concentrations from 0.2 μM to 600 μM; (B) fluorescence spectral of DHLA@N-CQDs probe with different concentrations H2O2; (C) Linear response relationship between fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of ·OH and H2O2; (D)Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and different concentrations of ·OH.
Fluorescence responses of DHLA@N-CQDs probe to different ROS/RNS (·OH, H2O2, ONOO−, 1O2, ClO−), various metal ions, common amino acids and their respective mixtures with H2O2.
The survival rate of HeLa cells with different concentrations of DHLA@N-CQDs after MTT test.
Fluorescence cell imaging of DHLA@N-CQDs and DHLA@N-CQDs/·OH in HeLa cells.
Sensing mechanism diagram of fluorescent switch sensor to hROS.
Comparison of the proposed method with different ·OH detection methods.
Determination and recovery results of H2O2 in human saliva samples.

head.ce:title: Redox-controlled fluorescent sensor for highly reactive oxygen species detection and cell imaging based on dihydrolipoic acid functionalized carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel redox-controlled fluorescent sensor for the detection of highly reactive oxygen species (hROS) was developed based on the unique green fluorescence emission of dihydrolipoic acid functionalized carbon quantum dots (DHLA@N-CQDs). Given the excellent redox characteristic between the disulfide bonds (S–S) and thiol (R-SH), the sensor was designed by bonding N-CQDs and DHLA under the action of coupling agents. When hROS was introduced into the sensing system, a large number of S–S gathered between DHLA@N-CQDs can effectively inhibit the fluorescence of probe. In addition, the as-prepared fluorescent sensor can sense hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in different response ranges, which ensures a high selectivity for hROS detection. Furthermore, the sensor system has been successfully utilized to detect the H2O2 in human saliva samples and the fluorescence imaging of ·OH in HeLa cells, exhibiting the feasibility of fluorescent sensors in practical applications.

Scheme 1. Sensing mechanism diagram of fluorescent switch sensor to hROS.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), a common term that defines the chemical substances that are produced in partial oxidation reaction of oxygen during the oxidative metabolism process of mitochondrial [1,2], is considered to be an indicator of oxidative stress and plays a significant role in cell signal transduction and pathological changes [3–7]. Among the abundance of ROS, ·OH and H2O2, which are regarded as the two most common highly reactive oxygen species (hROS) [8–11], have widely attracted the interest of researchers in the fields of chemistry and biomedicine due to their essential roles in the intercellular signaling and disease diagnosis [12–15]. There is a large number of studies have shown that different levels of ROS have different effects on organism cells. Lower levels of ROS may stimulate cell mitosis and proliferation. However, once the intracellular ROS content is higher than the normal level, the excessive accumulation of ROS could induce oxidative stress and lead to cell apoptosis and tissue injury [16,17]. Among them, ·OH is also considered the most aggressive hROS [18]. It has long been recognized that hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an indispensable chemical reagent [19], with strong oxidation, bleaching and sterilization capabilities, and has been widely used in many fields including food processing, medical research and environmental protection [20–22]. In addition, it is particularly noteworthy that H2O2 is also the most representative member of reactive oxygen species. Mounting studies have indicated that H2O2 plays a critical role in intercellular signal transduction, cell biochemistry, cellular immunity, and regulation of cellular physiological processes [23–26]. Nevertheless, the accumulation of high concentrations of H2O2 will break the balance of cell redox and damage the body's physiological system, which can increase the risk of many diseases including stroke, cancer, and Alzheimer's disease etc [13,15,22,27]. Therefore, high selectivity and accurate detection of the cancer level of hROS facilitates us to intimately diagnose and prevent various diseases closely related to hROS, which is of extremely essential for the health of the organism.

Up date, varied hROS analytical technologies, such as fluorescence [8], colorimetry [28], electrochemistry [29,30], and high performance liquid chromatography [31,32] has been reported. Most of those approaches involve inevitable shortcomings including specialized equipment and cumbersome analysis techniques. In addition, an increasing number of researchers are engrossed in enzyme biosensors for H2O2 sensing because it can be used as a substrate for many enzymatic reactions [33,34]. Moreover, the developed organic fluorescent probe for ·OH measurement is still interfered by other hROS, making the proposed method not highly selective [1,10]. At the same time, these methods are susceptible to environmental constraints, leading to instability and poor reproducibility [35,36]. In comparison to other analytical approaches, fluorescent probe technology has been successfully adopted in monitoring hROS and exhibits tremendous advantages because of its unique luminous properties, excellent biocompatibility, rapid response, and high temporal and spatial resolution [7,19,22,37]. Nevertheless, the vast majority of such sensors constructed by researchers are composed of non-biocompatible or toxic semiconductor quantum dots or designed organometallic framework materials [22,35,38–40], which greatly limits their practical application to a certain extent. Carbon quantum dots, with unique optical properties, low toxicity, easy surface functionalization, good biocompatibility and eco-friendliness, is a novel and eye-catching nanomaterial and has been widely used in the various fields of catalysis, nanomedicine, bioimaging and sensing [27,41–44]. The disadvantage is that unmodified or undoped carbon quantum dots have undesirable fluorescence quantum yield and short fluorescence emission wavelength due to the quantum size effect and surface defects, which greatly limits their application in cell imaging and other aspects [4,6,45,46]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop new fluorescent probes with high selectivity and accuracy for hROS sensing.

To address these issues and construct an attractive sensing strategy for the highly selective detection of hROS, we proposed a dihydrolipoic acid functionalized carbon quantum dots (DHLA@N-CQDs) fluorescence color sensor was adopted for specific sensing of hROS based on the unique features of disulfides and free radical scavenging mechanism of thiols [27,47,48]. The disulfide bonds (S–S) is an extremely notable function group because of its strong biological activity, excellent biocompatibility, and the advantage of being able to be effectively cleavage by small biological thiols or some specific medium reducing agents, and has attracted great interest of researchers in the field of biochemistry [27,48]. Although varied chemical sensors and biological applications based on the properties of S–S have been reported, it is still rare and unprecedented to use DHLA@N-CQDs probe for detecting hROS.

In previous work, we have successfully synthesized N-CQDs with the blue fluorescence emission. In this work, we designed a fluorescence analysis approach for ·OH and H2O2 sensing separately in different response ranges with DHLA@N-CQDs based on the properties of disulfide bonds. Furthermore, we modified the dihydrolipoic acid obtained by reducing lipoic acid with NaBH4 on the surface of the N-CQDs using a coupling agent to obtain DHLA@N-CQDs. Interestingly, the prepared DHLA@N-CQDs can emit green fluorescence at a larger wavelength. As shown in Scheme 1 , triggering DHLA@N-CQDs to exhibit green fluorescence emission due to the effective cleavage of disulfide bonds by NaBH4. However, the fluorescence signal of DHLA@N-CQDs is strongly quenched by a large number of S–S formed between DHLA@N-CQDs under hROS oxidation. The sensing scheme we constructed is simple, easy to implement, and utilized to the actual detection of H2O2 in human saliva samples and fluorescence imaging of ·OH in HeLa cells with satisfactory results.

Lipoic acid (ALA) and citric acid (CA) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China). Ethylenediamine (EDA) was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, China. Tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris), Hydrogen chloride (HCl), and Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) were Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaNO2, CaCl2, NaCl, NaClO and Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), were the products of Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Company. 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamine) propyl (EDC HCl) and n-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from GL Bio-chem Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glutathione (GSH), Thymine (T), l-Arginine (L-Arg), l-Histidine (L-His) and l-Threonine (L-Thr), (China National Pharmaceutical Group Corporation, Shanghai). Dialysis bags (cutoff molecular weight = 3000 Da) were from Union Car-bide Co. (USA). All chemicals and reagents are of at least analytical grade without any next purification. Secondary water (18.2 MΩ) was used for the configuration of all solutions throughout the experiment, which was offered by Milli-Q purification system (American).

A Cary 300 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Varian, USA) was applied to collect the UV–vis absorption data with a 1 cm path length cuvette. All fluorescence spectra were carried out with the LS-55 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The morphology feature of DHLA@N-CQDs was measured by using a Teanci G2 T20 transmission electron microscopy (FEI, USA) at a 200 kV accelerated voltage. A Nicolet 380 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer was applied to provide functional groups and chemical bond information of DHLA@N-CQDs. The elements on the surface of the synthesized fluorescent probe were obtained using a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA). Cell imaging was obtained at a Leica TCS SP5 II confocal laser microscope (Germany).

In previous work, a kind of N-CQDs with high blue fluorescence emission was synthesized. The quantum dots are simple to prepare, have good water solubility, and easy to modify, which can satisfy the needs of subsequent experiments. In short, the mixture formed by adding 2 g of CA and 1 mL of EDA to 40 mL of ultrapure water was transferred to a 100 mL reactor and heated for 2 h at 150 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained brown solution was dialyzed and purified in ultrapure water for 48 h.

The synthesis of DHLA@N-CQDs fluorescent probe carried out in two steps. First, 0.41 g of ALA was added to 18.4 mL of ultrapure water containing 200 μL NaBH4 (0.1 mM), stirred until the mixture was transparent and slightly yellow at room temperature. Subsequently, NHS (50 mg) and EDC (47 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL of 0.1 mg/mL N-CQDs, and stirred to fully activate the oxygen-containing groups of N-CQDs. After 40 min vigorous stirring, previously prepared DHLA was added under rapid stirring to ensure sufficient reaction. After reacting overnight, the obtained mixture was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a cutoff volume of 1000 Da for 36 h to remove unreacted reagents. Finally, DHLA@N-CQDs was obtained after freeze-drying.

The quantum yield (QY) of the DHLA@N-CQDs was calculated based on a previously research method [49]. In this paper, the absorption value of DHLA@N-CQDs and fluorescein was lower than 0.05 at 470 nm in order to minimize the self-absorption effect. Fluorescein in 0.1 M NaOH (QY = 95%) was used as a reference solution with excitation wavelength at 470 nm to determine and calculate QY of the DHLA@N-CQDs according to the following equation:
(1)
ϕ x
=
ϕ st
(
I x
/
I
s t
)
(
η x
2
/
η
s t
2
)
(
A
s t
/
A x
)
Where ϕ stands for the QY, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A refers to absorption values, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “x” and “st” correspond to the sample and the reference, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing procedure for detection of hROS
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run, 100 μL (10 μg/mL) of the prepared DHLA@N-CQDs stock solution were mixed with 250 μL PBS buffer (10 mM) at pH 7.4 in a series of 5 mL colorimetric tubes. Subsequently, varying concentrations of hROS were introduced into above solution and the mixture was diluted to 5 mL by adding distilled water. Among them, ·OH with varying concentrations were generated by adding different concentrations of Fe2+ and H2O2 (1:6). Finally, the fluorescence spectra at 480–750 nm emission wavelength range were measured under an excitation wavelength of 470 nm.

HeLa cells were chosen to culture in a 96-well plate, supplemented with 90% DMEM full culture solution containing 10% fetal bovine serum under 5% CO2 at 37 °C for the MTT toxicity test of DHLA@N-CQDs overnight in CO2 culture box. The medium was incubated for 24 h, followed by addition of DHLA@N-CQDs in the 10–60 μg/mL concentrations range for another 6 h. Then 20 μL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was introduced and further cultured for another 3 h at 37 °C. Afterwards, the plates were cleaned three times using PBS buffer. Finally, the absorbance value of mixed products was obtained at 470 nm by using a microplate reader. The cell viability (%), defined as the absorbance ratio in the presence of DHLA@N-CQDs, was calculated using equation (2), where A and A0 are the absorbance of the wells with and without DHLA@N-CQDs, respectively.
(2)
VR =
A
A 0
× 100 %

We designed a cell imaging experiment for evaluating the fluorescence sensing ability of the constructed system to ·OH at the cellular level. First, HeLa cells were cleaned several times with PBS buffer to remove the remaining fluorescent probe. For assessing ·OH uptake, subsequently, 200 μM ·OH (Fe2+:H2O2 = 1:6) was introduced into the culture medium and incubated for 6 h at 37 °C with DHLA@N-CQDs. Finally, followed by washing again with PBS three times, cell imaging was collected by a confocal fluorescence microscope with the excitation wavelength of 470 nm.
The DHLA@N-CQDs fluorescence nanoprobes were characterized by TEM, UV–vis, FTIR and XPS.

The morphology and size distribution of DHLA@N-CQDs were recorded via TEM. As depicted in Fig. 1 (A), it can be seen from the TEM micrograph of nanoprobes that DHLA@N-CQDs are quasi-spherical shape, with exceptional monodispersity no obvious agglomeration. The size range of DHLA@N-CQDs was distributed in 3.2–3.8 nm with an average diameter of 3.5 nm from the corresponding size distribution of nanoparticles. This size was slightly larger than undoped carbon quantum dots, which indicated that the modification of DHLA enlarged the original sizes of CQDs [50]. The inset HRTEM image in Fig. 1(A) reveals that the synthesized DHLA@N-CQDs have high crystallinity and resolvable lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, indicating that the newly formed DHLA@N-CQDs is homologous with the previously synthesized nanostructure of N-CQDs.

The FTIR was measured to analyze the functional groups present on the surface of the DHLA, N-CQDs, and DHLA@N-CQDs, respectively. As represented in Fig. 1(C), the FTIR spectrum of DHLA@N-CQDs exhibits two obvious peaks at 674.88 and 2538.56 cm−1, which were consistent with the characteristic peak of DHLA and corresponded to the bending vibration of C–S and stretching vibration band of S–H [51]. After functionalization of CQDs with DHLA, not only stretching vibration of N–H/C–H retained at 2969.46 cm−1, but also the typical peak at 1708.36 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of –COOH disappeared, whereas the stretching mode of CO in -CO-NH- (1650.75 cm−1) increased [52]. It can be attributed to the effective coupling and condensation of -CO-NH- covalent bond between CQDs and DHLA, which reveals the successful bonding of DHLA groups into the CQDs moiety. With the addition of hROS, the two peaks at 694.8 cm−1 and 2501.5 cm−1 disappeared in the spectrum. In their stead, a vibration band at 597.8 cm−1 appeared assigned to C–S and S–S bands of disulfide.

To further explore the surface chemical composition and chemical bonding, an analysis of the DHLA@N-CQDs surface elements was performed and recorded using the XPS technique. Fig. 2 (A) displays the full survey XPS spectrum of the DHLA@N-CQDs with four predominant peaks at 286.6 eV, 399.8 eV, 532.7 eV, and 162.9 eV and atomic percentages of approximately 50.8%, 9.5%, 30.17%, and 9.5%, which corresponds to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p respectively [53]. The high resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2(C) is deconvoluted into three component peaks with binding energies around 284.7 eV, 286.3 eV, 287.9 eV which are assigned to C–C/CC, C–N, and O–CO respectively. Fig. 2(D) displays N1s spectrum with two distinct peaks at 398.9 eV, 400.4 eV which corresponds to N–O and C–N bonds. Fig. 2(E) reveals that the XPS peaks for O1s indicate the existence of S–O (531.1 eV), N–O (532.6 eV), O–CO (535.4 eV) bonds. In addition, S2p spectrum can be deconvoluted into three major peaks centered at 161.1 eV, 163.1 eV, and 167.1 eV reflects the bonding structure of S–H, and C–S and S–O, which verifies the synthesized DHLA@N-CQDs having the S element. These results were consistent with the FTIR data. These results indicate that DHLA was retained in the DHLA@N-CQDs as the residues comprising C, N, O, and S originate from this precursor.

To further analyze the optical properties of DHLA@N-CQD, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum were confirmed. The green line represents the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the DHLA@N-CQDs, which exhibited two characteristic peaks at 258 and 335 nm corresponded to π-π* transition of the graphite sp2 domain and n-π* absorption band, respectively. More interesting, compared with unmodified simple N-CQDs, the N-CQDs functionalized with DHLA changed from the previous blue fluorescence to bright green luminescence under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamps, as it can be seen in the inset of Fig. 1(D). This strong fluorescence gives the promise application in fluorescence imaging. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3 (B), although fluorescence excitation wavelength changed from 420 to 510 nm, emission peak positions did not display obvious shift, further indicating that the excitation-independent fluorescence behavior of DHLA@N-CQDs originated from uniform size and surface deflects of nanoparticles. At the same time, the maximum emission of DHLA@N-CQDs is at 554 nm when excitation wavelength is 470 nm. All the above results suggest that the DHLA@N-CQDs have been satisfactorily synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of hROS fluorescence assay
ce:section.ce:para: We have conducted a more in-depth study on the mechanism of the highly selective measurement of hROS using the constructed fluorescence nanosensor based on properties of disulfide bonds (S–S) and special function of switching freely between thiol and disulfide. Lipoic acid is a small, highly reducing antioxidant molecule with abundant S–S. The S–S are easily cleaved to form dihydrolipoic acid and release a small part of free sulfhydryl groups and oxygen-containing groups under the action of NaBH4 strong reducing agent. At the time, the hydroxyl and amino groups on the N-CQDs with blue fluorescence emission will be covalently incorporated into the surface of N-CQDs with the carboxyl group on the dihydrolipoic acid through the amide linker, forming the DHLA@N-CQDs fluorescent nanoprobes with green fluorescence emission. Once hROS is introduced, the S–H groups from DHLA@N-CQDs will rapidly undergo redox reaction with hROS and transform into a non-fluorescent disulfide component to quench the fluorescence, which can be attributed to the effective elimination of hROS by thiol residues and the induction of a large number of S–S triggered by oxidation. Hence, we designed and synthesized a new fluorescent switch for the evaluation of hROS activity from the change of the fluorescence signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimize the experimental parameters of the sensing procedure
ce:section.ce:para: Taking the constructed probe for H2O2 sensing as a typical example, we further investigated the effects of some parameters related to the experiment conditions to obtain excellent sensitivity and high performance of nanosensor. The effect of several different buffer systems on fluorescence quenching efficiency of DHLA@N-CQDs/H2O2 was studied by measuring the fluorescence intensity of PBS and Tris-HCl buffer solutions mixed with the reaction system, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 (A), the fluorescence quenching efficiency of H2O2 on the system was as high as 62% compared with the influence of Tris-HCl buffer solution on the system after adding PBS buffer solution, which indicates that the choice of PBS will be conductive to interacting between H2O2 and DHLA@N-CQDs. The fluorescence quenching efficiency had hardly changed from 4.3 to 6.0 with increasing pH values of PBS suffer solutions, while increased in the range of 6.0–7.0 and reached the lowest fluorescence intensity between 7.0 and 9.0 and remained unchanged. This may be attributed to the fact that the carboxyl groups on the surface of DHLA@N-CQDs tend to be protonated when the pH is less than 6.0, which weakened the coupling of DHLA and N-CQDs and leaded to a decrease in quenching efficiency. Therefore, DHLA@N-CQDs nanoprobe might be useable under the physiological body fluids and cell environments with a pH of 7.4. In addition, we further discussed the influence of suitable incubation time on the quenching system. The fluorescence intensity of the system would gradually decrease with increasing time. The maximum of the quenching efficiency of hROS when the incubation time reaches 60 min. Thus, 60 min was selected as the best time for the recognition reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of hROS using the ON-OFF sensing behavior of DHLA@N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The process of using the constructed sensor to quantitatively analyze hROS is roughly divided into two steps. Under optimum conditions, the sensitivity of DHLA@N-CQDs fluorescent probe was studied for quantitative assays of H2O2. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (B), the fluorescence intensity of DHLA@N-CQDs at 554 nm decreases dramatically upon increasing the concentration of H2O2 from 0.02 to 90 mM. And the H2O2 concentration in the range of 0.02–20 mM and 20–90 mM was proportional to the fluorescence intensity of DHLA@N-CQDs. A fitted linear equation can be expressed as F0 = −5.70[H2O2]+659.81 and F0 = −2.89[H2O2]+601.13, of which the detection limit was estimated to be 0.005 mM at a signal-to-noise ratio 3. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5(D), the quantitative results of ·OH under the same conditions exhibited a good linear relationship in the range of 0.2–600 μM (R2 = 0.995), with a detection limit of 0.12 μM. At the same time, Fig. 5(C) showed that the sensing platform we built can sense ·OH and H2O2 in different linear response ranges. Besides, a comparison of analysis result of hROS was displayed in Table 1
among the reported methods in terms of detection limit and linear range [1,9,10,16,18,54], which showed that the established method has a wider linear range compared with other reported fluorescent probes and is close to electrochemical sensors [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference study for hROS detection
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of the proposed sensing platform to hROS, a wide series of biological relevant interferences towards hROS including numerous metal ions 100 mM (Na+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cl−), 50 mM common amino acids (L-Arg, L-Thr, T-His), Thymine, NaNO2, GSH and ROS/RNS (500 μM ·OH, 10 mM H2O2, 10 mM ONOO−, ClO−, 1O2) was examined under the optimum conditions [56]. It is observed that Fig. 6 (A) displays almost negligible fluorescence quenching as the addition of other interfering substrates, while only ·OH and H2O2 would cause a significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity. Although the designed nanoprobe responds to both, it can be selectively measured for ·OH and H2O2 due to the different response ranges. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of DHLA@N-CQDs sensing system had little impact on monitoring of hROS after adding the metal ions and other interfering substrates co-excited with hROS, respectively. These results evidenced that the DHLA@N-CQDs nanoprobe exhibited outstanding selectivity to hROS with the existence of various analytes and could be satisfied for the sensing of hROS in the complex biological and environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting H2O2 in human saliva samples
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the H2O2 content in human saliva samples and the recovery experiments of spiked samples in order to determine the feasibility of the designed DHLA@N-CQDs probe in practical application. Saliva samples from two healthy volunteers were collected, centrifuged at 6000 r/min for 15 min, and the supernatant was collected for use [55]. Under optimal conditions, different concentrations of H2O2 were added to the saliva samples, and the changes in the fluorescence intensity of the system were recorded. The recovery results show that the DHLA@N-CQDs fluorescent probe is suitable for the determination of H2O2 in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity test of DHLA@N-CQDs and its cellular imaging application
ce:section.ce:para: A standard MTT test was implemented by incubating HeLa cells with increasing concentration of DHLA@N-CQDs probes for 24 h to evaluate the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of constructed DHLA@N-CQDs fluorescent probe for applications in intracellular environment imaging and clinical medicine. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , the relative cell viability of HeLa cells can still be maintained above 80% without obvious cell damage after incubating with higher concentrations of DHLA@N-CQDs for 6 h, which suggests that the DHLA@N-CQDs fluorescence nanoprobe we prepared not only have unique characteristics optical properties, and suitable for further research and application in the cell environment due to its relatively low toxicity and good biocompatibility.

Continuously, 20 μg/mL DHLA@N-CQDs and HeLa cells were cultured at 37 °C for 6 h and treated with 200 μM ·OH for 120 min based on the results of DHLA@N-CQDs nanoprobe MTT test proved low toxicity and objective biocompatibility to explore their potential applications in biological imaging, and the final fluorescence image was recorded at the emission wavelength of 554 nm using laser confocal microscopy. As shown in Fig. 8 , with the addition of a certain concentration of ·OH, the color of HeLa cells that originally had green fluorescent emission gradually weakened, and showed almost negligible faint green light with the extension of time. The above results all prove the possibility of DHLA@N-CQDs application in biological imaging.

In summary, a novel fluorescent sensor for hROS detection has been proposed in different linear response ranges, which is realized by the first use of DHLA to functionalize N-CQDs. The presented DHLA@N-CQDs fluorescent probe exhibits low toxicity, excellent optical characteristics, high selectivity, strong anti-interference ability, and 21.4% quantum yield. The new approach shows a wide linear range of 0.02–90 mM for the determination of H2O2, which is almost comparable to electrochemical sensors. In addition, the determination of ·OH also shows a wide response range of 0.2–600 μM and a low detection limit of 0.12 μM. Meanwhile, this fluorescent sensor with good analytical performance has been satisfactorily applied for the determination of H2O2 content in human saliva samples and bioimaging of ·OH in HeLa cells, which may provide a reference for the research of other redox substances in biological systems.
Ruxia Zhang: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Writing - Review & Editing. Zhefeng Fan: Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2021.117998.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A pyridine-Si-rhodamine-based near-infrared fluorescent probe for visualizing reactive oxygen species in living cells
Spectrochim. Acta Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
